title: The collection-specific TrueForAll method should be preferred instead of the All extension
description: The collection-specific "TrueForAll" method should be used instead of the "All" extensionAnalyze your codeintentionality-efficientmaintainabilityCode SmellperformanceWhy is this an issue?How can I fix it?More InfoBoth theList.TrueForAllmethod and theIEnumerable.Allmethod can be used to check if all list elements satisfy a given condition in a collection. However,List.TrueForAllcan be faster thanIEnumerable.AllforListobjects. The performance difference may be minor for small collections, but for large collections, it can be noticeable.It is important to enable this rule with caution, as performance outcomes can vary significantly across different runtimes.
recommendation: Replace with the recommended alternative approach.

noncompliant:
List<int> numbers = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 };
bool allPositive = numbers.All(x => x > 0); // Noncompliant - use TrueForAll for List<T>
compliant:
List<int> numbers = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 };
bool allPositive = numbers.TrueForAll(x => x > 0); // Compliant - using collection-specific method

checks.message: Use TrueForAll instead of All extension method for List<T> collections for better performance

never_trigger_on: IEnumerable collections that are not List<T>
logic.reasoning: Detects calls to .All() LINQ extension on List<T> types where TrueForAll() would be more efficient. Excludes logging and comments where this is safe.

should_trigger: List<int> numbers = new List<int>(); bool result = numbers.All(x => x > 0);
var items = new List<string>(); items.All(s => s.Length > 0);
